schools.summary: The giving of the Passover ordinance — 'in the land of Egypt' (the command given while Israel is still in bondage, before the firstborn are slain, appointed for the very night of redemption); a divine consecration needed, that the outward severance from Egypt be joined to an inward severance from all that was heathen, marking them God's redeemed people (Keil and Delitzsch); the section reading as a part of the Law (Ellicott); a solemn symbol and memorial of their redemption (Calvin); spoken before the darkness but recorded here, that the plagues' account not be interrupted (Wesley).

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron
in the land of Egypt
the Passover ordinance
redemption framed by a rite

schools.summary: The new reckoning of the year — 'this month shall be the beginning of months' (Abib, later Nisan, the month of the Exodus, set as the first, answering to part of March and April); the change — hitherto they began the year in autumn near the equinox (Tisri, the civil reckoning), now in spring, at least for the sacred calendar, dating from their deliverance (Ellicott, Wesley); not only the first but the chief of months, made famous by their coming out of Egypt, and more so by the Messiah's death in this season (Gill); the redemption so great a mercy it began a new era, time reckoned from the act of salvation.

themes: the beginning of months
the first month of the year
redemption resets the calendar
a new era

schools.summary: The taking of the lamb — 'in the tenth day... every man a lamb, a lamb for an house' (each family setting apart a lamb on the tenth of Abib); the timing peculiar to this first Passover, the lamb taken on the tenth and kept till the fourteenth, in later observances taken nearer the day (Clarke); the four days allowing careful inspection that the animal be proved free from blemish (Ellicott); the household character — each family its own lamb, the feast domestic and national; Calvin's question why a lamb in every house when one Lamb reconciles the Church — the one sacrifice of Christ personally appropriated by each household and soul.

themes: all the congregation of Israel
every man a lamb
a lamb for an house
the one Lamb personally applied

schools.summary: The provision for small households — 'if the household be too little for the lamb, let him and his neighbour next unto his house take it' (two neighbouring households joining over one lamb where a family was too small to eat it at one sitting); the measure 'according to the number of the souls... every man according to his eating' (reckoning by who would partake, that the lamb be wholly consumed and none wasted); the rabbinic detail of not fewer than ten persons nor more than twenty (Clarke); smallness no bar, the neighbour's house supplying what the lamb required; the communal warmth, neighbour joined to neighbour.

themes: if the household be too little
his neighbour next unto his house
according to the number of the souls
none excluded for fewness

schools.summary: The unblemished lamb, a type of Christ — 'without blemish, a male of the first year... from the sheep, or from the goats' (tamim, perfect, free of every bodily fault, in the vigour of its first year, a kid as well as a lamb, Lev 22:19); the demand for perfection teaching we give God our best, not the blind or lame (Mal 1:8, Ellicott), the expiation not legitimate unless without fault (Calvin); the typology — the spotless lamb prefiguring Christ, the Lamb 'without blemish and without spot' (1 Pet 1:19), the sinless Saviour offered for sin; the perfection of the paschal lamb pointing to the moral perfection of Christ, the only fit offering.

themes: without blemish
a male of the first year
of the sheep or the goats
type of the sinless Christ

schools.summary: The keeping and killing of the lamb — 'keep it up until the fourteenth day' (the lamb taken on the tenth, kept and tended apart four days before sacrifice, peculiar to this first Passover, never commanded again; held in custody for inspection, proved without blemish, Ellicott); 'kill it in the evening' (literally 'between the two evenings,' the late afternoon toward sunset); 'the whole assembly... shall kill it' (each household slaying its own lamb, yet all as one congregation at the same hour, personal and corporate); the typology — the keeping picturing Christ's preservation till his appointed sufferings, crucified on this very day at the season the lambs were slain (Gill).

themes: keep it up until the fourteenth day
kill it in the evening
the whole assembly
Christ slain at the appointed time

schools.summary: The blood upon the doorposts — 'take of the blood, and strike it on the two side posts and on the upper door post' (some of the lamb's blood, caught in a basin, struck or sprinkled by a bunch of hyssop, Ex 12:22, Clarke); the door the place the destroying influence would enter, the blood above and beside it marking the entrance protected (Ellicott); 'wherein they shall eat it' (the blood outside, the lamb within, the same households sheltered and feasting); the typology — the blood on the door an emblem of the blood of Christ shed for sin and applied to the believer, sheltering the soul from judgment; not enough that the lamb be slain, its blood must be applied, the personal appropriation of the sacrifice.

themes: take of the blood
strike it on the two side posts
the upper door post
the blood of Christ applied

schools.summary: The eating of the lamb — 'in that night, roast with fire, and unleavened bread; and with bitter herbs' (the lamb eaten the same night, roasted whole; roasting the simplest mode and the only one open to all, lacking cauldrons to boil a whole lamb, Ellicott); the unleavened bread signifying the haste of departure and the purging of old leaven; the bitter herbs a memorial of the bitterness of their bondage, the sweetness of the lamb eaten with the bitterness of servitude remembered (Gill); the typology — the bitter herbs answering to repentance and the sense of sin's bitterness in feeding on Christ; the eating, not only the slaying, belonging to the rite.

themes: eat the flesh in that night
roast with fire
unleavened bread and bitter herbs
the bitterness of bondage

schools.summary: The lamb roasted whole — 'Eat not of it raw, nor sodden with water, but roast with fire' (not eaten underdone or boiled but only roasted, perhaps set apart from heathen customs of raw or boiled flesh, Wesley/Clarke); 'his head with his legs, and with the purtenance' (roasted entire, not cut up, the entrails not removed and abused for divination as the heathen did, Clarke); the typology — as not a bone of the paschal lamb was broken (Ex 12:46), so not a bone of Christ was broken on the cross (John 19:36), the type fulfilled in the Lamb of God; the ancient note (Justin Martyr) of the lamb spitted on two crossed pieces of wood forming a cross.

themes: eat not of it raw
nor sodden with water
roast with fire, whole
not a bone broken

schools.summary: Nothing of the lamb to remain — 'let nothing of it remain until the morning; that which remaineth ye shall burn with fire' (the lamb entirely eaten that night, the rest destroyed not kept); the reasons — in a hot land the flesh would putrefy, and what was offered to God should not be subject to corruption, the type that Christ's body saw no corruption (Clarke); nothing to remain lest it be profaned or put to superstitious use, degraded into a relic (Ellicott); the typology — eating the whole lamb, leaving nothing, picturing the receiving of a whole Christ by faith, in both natures and all offices, fed upon entirely (Gill); guarding against both waste and superstition.

themes: let nothing remain until the morning
burn with fire the remainder
the whole lamb consumed
a whole Christ received

schools.summary: The posture of haste and the name — 'loins girded, shoes on your feet, staff in your hand' (the long garments tucked up for travel, sandals on not the bare feet of rest, staff as for a march, all the marks of pilgrims poised to set out, Clarke); 'eat it in haste' (a hurried meal, fitting men about to be summoned out of Egypt); these injunctions referring to the impending departure, not observed at later Passovers (Ellicott); 'it is the LORD's passover' (a sign of God's passing over Israel's houses when he destroyed the Egyptians, the rite named from the sparing mercy, Wesley); the pilgrim posture befitting the redeemed, ready to leave the world, feeding on the Lamb as those who do not abide here.

themes: loins girded, shoes on, staff in hand
eat it in haste
it is the LORD's passover
the pilgrim posture

schools.summary: The judgment on Egypt and its gods — 'I will pass through the land of Egypt this night' (the manifestation of his powerful presence, consistent with his omnipresence, Gill); 'smite all the firstborn... both man and beast' (the tenth and crowning plague); 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment' ('gods' preferable to 'princes,' Ellicott; since the Egyptians held animals sacred, slaying the firstborn of their beasts a judgment on their gods, Clarke; God declaring himself judge against the false gods, whose impotence then appeared, Calvin); 'I am the LORD' (the seal of his authority); the deliverance of Israel at once the judgment of Egypt and the overthrow of its idols.

themes: I will pass through the land of Egypt
smite all the firstborn
judgment against all the gods of Egypt
salvation and judgment together

schools.summary: The blood as the token of sparing — 'the blood shall be to you for a token' (better 'a token for you,' a token to God on the people's behalf, Ellicott; the sign to the destroying angel that the house is under God's protection, none to be harmed, Clarke); 'when I see the blood, I will pass over you' (God regarding the sprinkled blood and at the sight passing over the marked house, the plague not falling on Israel); it is the blood God looks upon, not the worthiness of the people, who were sinners like the Egyptians, the blood making the difference; the typology — as God passed over the blood-marked houses, so the blood of Christ shelters the believer from judgment; the gospel in figure.

themes: the blood shall be to you for a token
when I see the blood
I will pass over you
salvation through the blood

schools.summary: The perpetual memorial — 'this day shall be unto you for a memorial' (keeping alive the remembrance of God's justice on Egypt and mercy on Israel, of both his severity and goodness, Clarke); 'keep it a feast to the LORD... by an ordinance for ever' (an annual festival with solemn religious joy throughout their generations); 'for ever' denoting perpetuity, yet a perpetuity belonging to a type, fulfilled in Christ, the figure enduring till the substance comes (Calvin); the Passover continued and fulfilled in the Eucharist, 'Christ our passover is sacrificed for us' (1 Cor 5:7), the Church's Easter answering to the paschal season (Ellicott); a redeemed people keeping their redemption in remembrance.

themes: this day for a memorial
keep it a feast to the LORD
an ordinance for ever
Christ our Passover

schools.summary: The feast of unleavened bread — 'seven days shall ye eat unleavened bread' (from the fifteenth to the twenty-first, a week with all leaven put away, a distinct ordinance joined to the Passover, Clarke/Gill); 'put away leaven out of your houses' (removed from the very start, the houses cleansed); 'whosoever eateth leavened bread... shall be cut off from Israel' (the penalty guarding the command, Calvin); the typology — leaven, which spreads and corrupts, a figure of sin and the 'old leaven' of malice, to be purged, keeping the feast with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth (1 Cor 5:7-8); the deliverance issuing in holiness; also commemorating the haste that left no time to leaven the bread.

themes: seven days unleavened bread
put away leaven
cut off from Israel
purge the old leaven

schools.summary: The holy convocations — 'in the first day... and in the seventh day... an holy convocation' (the opening and closing days of the feast kept as holy days, the people gathered for worship); the first place in Scripture of an assembly collected purely for religious worship, a 'holy convocation,' the redeemed coming together to worship the God who saved them (Clarke); 'no manner of work... save that which every man must eat' (kept as sabbaths, free from ordinary labour, with the one exception of preparing the festal food, Ellicott); the feast not only a domestic remembrance but a gathered public act of worship, framed by sacred assembly and rest.

themes: an holy convocation
the first and seventh day
no manner of work
framed by worship

schools.summary: The ground of the feast in the Exodus — 'ye shall observe the feast of unleavened bread' (the seven days enjoined again, to be diligently marked); 'for in this selfsame day have I brought your armies out of Egypt' (the feast tied to the very day God led Israel out); 'armies'/'hosts' applied to Israel (Ex 6:26), the people going out not as a rabble but as marshalled hosts under God's leading (Ellicott); 'the selfsame day,' literally 'in the body or strength of the day,' suggesting they began the march about daybreak (Clarke); the feast rooted in a real, dated act of God, not a seasonal custom, binding each generation to the founding deliverance.

themes: observe the feast of unleavened bread
in this selfsame day
brought your armies out
rooted in the Exodus

schools.summary: The exact bounds of the feast — 'in the first month, on the fourteenth day at even... until the one and twentieth day at even' (the eating of unleavened bread fixed from one evening to another, seven days exactly); the evening intended at the start not that with which the fourteenth began but the one with which it closed, the end of the fourteenth and beginning of the fifteenth (Ellicott); the season precisely appointed by the calendar God reset (v2), the 'first month' being Abib/Nisan now the head of the year (Gill); the precision guarding the ordinance and ensuring all Israel keeps the feast together at the same time in every generation; the bounds evening to evening, by Hebrew reckoning.

themes: the fourteenth day at even
until the twenty-first at even
exact bounds
a common calendar

schools.summary: The strict purging of leaven — 'seven days shall there be no leaven found in your houses' (not merely none eaten but none even found; the later Jews instituting a most rigorous search through every part of their houses, removing and destroying all leaven, Clarke); 'whosoever eateth... shall be cut off' (the solemn excision for the breach); 'whether he be a stranger, or born in the land' (the 'stranger' not the heathen but the foreigner adopted, circumcised, a full proselyte, bound by the same ordinance, while unconverted heathens had no part in the Passover, Wesley/Ellicott); the typology — the searching out of every particle of leaven figuring the diligent purging of all sin from heart and life.

themes: no leaven found in your houses
shall be cut off
stranger or born in the land
searching, universal cleansing

schools.summary: The summary prohibition of leaven — 'ye shall eat nothing leavened' (not only leavened bread but anything containing leaven forbidden during the seven days, the rule admitting no exception of kind, Gill); 'in all your habitations shall ye eat unleavened bread' (wherever an Israelite dwelt, in every house, only unleavened bread, the command reaching the whole people in all their homes); restating and sealing the law, leaving no room for evasion; the repetition pressing the matter's importance; the typology — the total exclusion of leaven from every dwelling answering to the believer's call to be wholly cleansed from sin, not one corner harbouring the old leaven.

themes: eat nothing leavened
in all your habitations
unleavened bread alone
total purity

schools.summary: Moses conveying the command to the elders — 'Moses called for all the elders of Israel' (directed to speak to all the congregation, v3, he does so mediately through the elders, heads of families and principal men, in office not merely age, who carry it to the rest, Ellicott/Gill); Moses a faithful steward teaching Israel all God commanded, as fully as he received it (Henry); 'Draw out and take you a lamb... and kill the passover' ('draw out'/'withdraw,' mashak intransitive, bidding them fetch a lamb for each household; the lamb here called 'the passover' itself, the sacrifice named by the institution, Clarke); the orderly conveyance from God to Moses to elders to families.

themes: Moses called for the elders
draw out and take you a lamb
kill the passover
conveyed through the heads

schools.summary: The applying of the blood and staying within — 'a bunch of hyssop' (a small plant, perhaps the caper growing plentifully in the Sinai region, fit for sprinkling, Ellicott) 'dipped in the blood in the basin' and used to strike the lintel and two side-posts; the hyssop, associated with cleansing, the means of applying the atoning blood; 'none of you shall go out at the door until the morning' (the Israelites kept within their blood-marked houses through the night, lest they mix with the Egyptians and be exposed, learning they too were liable to judgment but for the blood, Calvin); safety within the blood, to step outside courting the destroyer.

themes: a bunch of hyssop
dip it in the blood
strike the lintel and side posts
none go out till morning

schools.summary: The LORD's passing over and the barring of the destroyer — 'the LORD will pass through to smite the Egyptians' (going through to strike the firstborn); 'when he seeth the blood... the LORD will pass over the door' (at the sight of the sprinkled blood sparing the marked house, the blood seen and accepted the ground of sparing); 'will not suffer the destroyer to come in' (the plague personified as 'the destroyer,' named also in Heb 11:28, an angel employed by God as agent); the typology — the blood of Christ, seen by God, turning away the judgment due the believer, the destroyer barred from the soul sheltered by the Lamb; God himself keeping the door.
Wesley: whether 'the destroyer' was a good or an evil angel, we have not light to determine — God's instrument either way.

themes: the LORD will pass through to smite
when he seeth the blood
the destroyer barred
the blood turns the stroke aside

schools.summary: The perpetual obligation — 'ye shall observe this thing for an ordinance to thee and to thy sons for ever' (the feast laid not only on the present generation but on their children, throughout their history); the careful distinction — 'this thing' not the striking of the blood on the doorposts, peculiar to this first Passover and never repeated, but the sacrifice and feast of the lamb, the substance that was to continue (Ellicott, Gill); the saving application of the blood belonging to the unique night, the annual commemoration perpetual; the precept of annual celebration repeated, that the memory never perish (Calvin); the obligation passing down the covenant line, each generation owning the redemption.

themes: observe this thing for an ordinance
to thee and to thy sons for ever
the lasting feast, not the blood-rite
each generation bound

schools.summary: The keeping of the rite in the promised land — 'when ye be come to the land which the LORD will give you, according as he hath promised' (Canaan already given by promise, the land Israel would possess in a few years, Gill; the feast enjoined for the time of their settled inheritance); the continuity — the rite born in Egypt carried forward and kept in the land of rest, the redeemed commemorating their redemption ever after; once in the land, the recollection of deliverance yearly revived by this service, lest the memory fade with prosperity (Calvin); guarding against the forgetfulness of generations secure in the land; God tying the gift of the land to the remembrance of the redemption.

themes: when ye be come to the land
according as he hath promised
ye shall keep this service
from Egypt into the inheritance

schools.summary: The children's question and the rite's teaching — 'when your children shall say unto you, What mean ye by this service?' (the strange and solemn rite — the killing and roasting of the lamb, the bitter herbs, the unleavened bread — naturally moving the children to ask its meaning, Gill); the wisdom of it — the annual feast a most wise provision to preserve the memory of the deliverance, feasts and rites from antiquity keeping alive great events, a rite whose very strangeness prompts the young to inquire and the old to instruct (Clarke); the question an opportunity to recount the Exodus and the redemption by blood; the feast a standing catechism, handing down the faith within the family.

themes: when your children shall say
What mean ye by this service?
the rite teaches the children
a standing catechism

schools.summary: The explanation of the Passover and the people's worship: 'that ye shall say, It is the sacrifice of the LORD's passover, who passed over the houses of the children of Israel in Egypt, when he smote the Egyptians, and delivered our houses. And the people bowed the head and worshipped' — the answer to the children's question (v.26): the Passover the sacrifice commemorating the LORD's passing over the houses of Israel (marked with the blood) when he smote the Egyptians' firstborn (the meaning of the feast, the deliverance by the blood, the catechesis, the redemption explained to the children); the LORD's 'passing over' (sparing the blood-marked houses while smiting Egypt) — the deliverance by the blood of the lamb (a type of Christ, the blood that shelters from the destroyer, 1 Corinthians 5:7; Hebrews 11:28, the redemption commemorated); 'the people bowed the head and worshipped' (the people's reverent response, bowing in worship and thanksgiving for the promised deliverance, the faith that received the word and worshipped, Hebrews 11:28, 'through faith he kept the passover,' the gratitude and submission of the redeemed) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: That ye shall say, It is the sacrifice of the LORD's passover, who passed over the houses of the children of Israel in Egypt... And the people bowed the head and worshipped
the explanation of the Passover and the people's worship
the deliverance by the blood of the lamb, a type of Christ (1 Corinthians 5:7)
the faith that received the word and worshipped (Hebrews 11:28)

schools.summary: Israel's obedience to the Passover command: 'and the children of Israel went away, and did as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron, so did they' — the people obeying, doing all as the LORD had commanded (keeping the Passover, vv.1-27: taking the lamb, striking the blood, eating the flesh, the prompt obedience of faith, the keeping of the ordinance, the faith that obeyed, Hebrews 11:28); the obedience (doing all as commanded) — the faith that kept the Passover (the prompt compliance, the people's obedience); 'so did they' (the full obedience, exactly as commanded, the keeping of the ordinance, the faith of the redeemed, the blood applied, the deliverance secured, vv.13, the providence, the obedience that brought the deliverance) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the children of Israel went away, and did as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron, so did they
Israel's obedience to the Passover command
the prompt obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:28)
the blood applied, the deliverance secured (v.13)

schools.summary: The death of the firstborn at midnight: 'and it came to pass, that at midnight the LORD smote all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sat on his throne unto the firstborn of the captive that was in the dungeon; and all the firstborn of cattle' — the great stroke — 'at midnight the LORD smote all the firstborn in the land of Egypt': at the appointed hour (Exodus 11:4), the LORD (by the destroying angel, v.23; Hebrews 11:28) smiting every firstborn in Egypt (the tenth and climactic plague executed, the judgment foretold, Exodus 11:5, the immediate hand of God, the LORD himself, the destroyer passing through Egypt, the awful midnight stroke, the dead of night, the land asleep, the sudden universal death); the sweep of the judgment — from the highest (Pharaoh's firstborn, the heir of the throne) to the lowest (the captive in the dungeon, the meanest slave, none exempt, the leveling judgment of God, no rank or station spared, Exodus 11:5, the universal stroke, every house in Egypt with its dead, v.30); 'and all the firstborn of cattle' (even the firstborn of the beasts smitten, the judgment on the cattle the Egyptians worshipped, Exodus 12:12, 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment,' the complete judgment, the recompense for Pharaoh's slaying of Israel's sons, Exodus 1:22, the doom for refusing God's firstborn, Exodus 4:22-23, the providence, the last plague that broke Pharaoh, freed Israel) — Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the death of the firstborn execute?

sides.position: The tenth and climactic judgment - the LORD himself (by the destroyer, v.23; Hebrews 11:28) smiting at the appointed hour; the universal stroke from highest to lowest, the judgment on Egypt's gods (Exodus 12:12), the recompense for the slaying of Israel's sons (Exodus 1:22), the doom for refusing God's firstborn (Exodus 4:22-23)

themes: And it came to pass, that at midnight the LORD smote all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sat on his throne unto the firstborn of the captive that was in the dungeon; and all the firstborn of cattle
the death of the firstborn at midnight (the crux of the last plague executed)
the climactic judgment by the LORD's own hand, the universal stroke (Exodus 11:4-5; 12:23)
the judgment on Egypt's gods (12:12), the recompense and the doom (1:22; 4:22-23)

schools.summary: The great cry of bereaved Egypt: 'and Pharaoh rose up in the night, he, and all his servants, and all the Egyptians; and there was a great cry in Egypt; for there was not a house where there was not one dead' — Pharaoh and all Egypt rising in the night, roused by the sudden death (the universal alarm, the whole land awakened to find its dead, the great cry, the wail of grief foretold, Exodus 11:6, the terror of the bereaved land); the great cry (the universal lamentation, Exodus 11:6) — the anguish of the judgment (the wail of every house); 'for there was not a house where there was not one dead' (every house in Egypt bereaved, the firstborn of each family slain, v.29, the universal death, none exempt, from palace to dungeon, the magnitude of the judgment, the providence, the cry that broke Pharaoh's obstinacy, v.31) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Pharaoh rose up in the night... and there was a great cry in Egypt; for there was not a house where there was not one dead
the great cry of bereaved Egypt
the universal mourning, every house bereaved (Exodus 11:6; v.29)
the magnitude of the judgment breaking the king (v.31)

schools.summary: Pharaoh at last thrusting Israel out: 'and he called for Moses and Aaron by night, and said, Rise up, and get you forth from among my people, both ye and the children of Israel; and go, serve the LORD, as ye have said' — Pharaoh, broken by the death of his firstborn, summoning Moses and Aaron by night and ordering Israel out (the complete surrender, the proud king at last yielding, indeed urging them to go, the reversal, the king who said 'see my face no more,' Exodus 10:28, now summoning them, who said 'Who is the LORD?', Exodus 5:2, now bidding them serve the LORD); the full release (all the people, no compromise now) — the complete yielding (the king granting all he had refused: all the people, all their substance, v.32, the broken obstinacy); 'go, serve the LORD, as ye have said' (Pharaoh granting the very demand he had so long resisted, 'Let my people go, that they may serve me,' Exodus 5:1, the surrender, God's strong hand forcing the king, Exodus 6:1, the deliverance won) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And he called for Moses and Aaron by night, and said, Rise up, and get you forth from among my people... and go, serve the LORD, as ye have said
Pharaoh at last thrusting Israel out
the broken king granting all he had refused (Exodus 5:1; 10:28)
God's strong hand forcing the surrender, the deliverance won (Exodus 6:1)

schools.summary: Pharaoh granting the cattle and begging a blessing: 'also take your flocks and your herds, as ye have said, and be gone; and bless me also' — Pharaoh now granting even the flocks and herds (which he had refused, Exodus 10:24-26), bidding them be gone, and even begging 'bless me also' (the complete surrender, all the substance now granted, the humbling of the proud king, begging a blessing of the men he had defied); the flocks and herds granted (the very thing refused, Exodus 10:24) — the full release (the broken king yielding all); 'and bless me also' (Pharaoh begging a blessing, asking Moses to pray for him, or to bless him, the proud king humbled to entreat the deliverers, the abasement of the obstinate, the king who said 'Who is the LORD?', Exodus 5:2, now begging a blessing, the providence, the proud humbled) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB.

themes: Also take your flocks and your herds, as ye have said, and be gone; and bless me also
Pharaoh granting the cattle and begging a blessing
the broken king yielding all he had refused (Exodus 10:24)
the proud abased to entreat the deliverers (Exodus 5:2)

schools.summary: The Egyptians urging Israel to depart in haste: 'and the Egyptians were urgent upon the people, that they might send them out of the land in haste; for they said, We be all dead men' — the Egyptians (not only Pharaoh) urgently pressing Israel to leave quickly, fearing further death (the haste, the Egyptians thrusting them out, Exodus 11:1, the terror, the people fearing they would all die if Israel stayed, the reversal, the Egyptians, who had held Israel in bondage, now begging them to go); the urgency (the Egyptians hurrying Israel out) — the haste of the deliverance (the Passover eaten in haste, Exodus 12:11, now the departure in haste, the thrusting-out, Exodus 11:1); 'We be all dead men' (the Egyptians' terror, fearing total destruction if Israel remained, the dread of the judgment, the providence, the terror forcing the haste, the Egyptians driving out the people they had enslaved) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the Egyptians were urgent upon the people, that they might send them out of the land in haste; for they said, We be all dead men
the Egyptians urging Israel to depart in haste
the terror forcing the haste (Exodus 11:1; 12:11)
the oppressors driving out the people they had enslaved

schools.summary: The unleavened dough carried in haste: 'and the people took their dough before it was leavened, their kneadingtroughs being bound up in their clothes upon their shoulders' — the people, hurried out (v.33), taking their dough before it had risen (unleavened), wrapping their kneading-troughs in their clothes upon their shoulders (the haste of the departure, no time to leaven the bread, vv.39, the unleavened bread of the Exodus, the memorial of the haste, the feast of unleavened bread, Exodus 12:15-20; 13:3-7); the unleavened dough (taken before it rose, for haste) — the memorial of the hasty deliverance (the unleavened bread, the type of sincerity and truth, 1 Corinthians 5:8); 'their kneadingtroughs being bound up in their clothes upon their shoulders' (the people carrying their troughs and dough as they went, the haste, no time to bake, the providence, the unleavened bread a lasting memorial, the feast, Exodus 13:3, the marching-out of the people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the people took their dough before it was leavened, their kneadingtroughs being bound up in their clothes upon their shoulders
the unleavened dough carried in haste
the memorial of the hasty deliverance (Exodus 12:39; 13:3)
the unleavened bread, the type of sincerity and truth (1 Corinthians 5:8)

schools.summary: Israel asking the Egyptians for jewels and raiment: 'and the children of Israel did according to the word of Moses; and they borrowed of the Egyptians jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment' — the people doing as Moses had commanded (Exodus 11:2), asking the Egyptians for jewels of silver and gold and raiment (the 'borrowed,' Hebrew, asked, requested, not defrauded — the asking, the Egyptians freely giving, v.36, the spoiling of Egypt foretold, Genesis 15:14); the obedience (as commanded, Exodus 11:2) — the people asking (the obeying of the command); 'jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment' (the wealth of Egypt asked and given, the spoiling, v.36; Genesis 15:14, the recompense for the bondage, the wages of long unpaid servitude, the providence, the gold and silver later for the tabernacle, Exodus 25:2-7; 35:5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the children of Israel did according to the word of Moses; and they borrowed of the Egyptians jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment
Israel asking the Egyptians for jewels and raiment
'borrowed' means 'asked' - the Egyptians freely giving (v.36)
the recompense for the bondage, the spoiling foretold (Genesis 15:14)

schools.summary: The spoiling of the Egyptians: 'and the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians, so that they lent unto them such things as they required. And they spoiled the Egyptians' — God giving Israel favor with the Egyptians, so that they freely gave (the word 'lent' better 'gave,' for they did not expect return) the jewels and raiment asked (the providence, God inclining the oppressors' hearts to give, the spoiling by favor, not fraud); the Egyptians 'lent' (gave) all they required — the free giving (the older charge of fraudulent borrowing answered: God gave favor, the Egyptians gave freely, glad to be rid of Israel, v.33, the willing gift); 'they spoiled the Egyptians' — Israel 'spoiling' (despoiling, stripping) Egypt of its wealth (the fulfillment of the promise to Abraham, 'afterward shall they come out with great substance,' Genesis 15:14, 'they shall spoil them that spoiled them,' cf. Ezekiel 39:10, the just recompense, the wages of long, unpaid, cruel bondage, paid at last, the oppressed enriched by the oppressors, the providence — God's people not going out empty, but laden with the wealth of Egypt, the gold and silver to adorn the tabernacle, Exodus 35:5, a type of the spoiling of the strong man, the plunder of the kingdom of darkness, Colossians 2:15; Luke 11:22, the recompense of the redeemed) — Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the spoiling of the Egyptians signify?

sides.position: The fulfillment of the promise to Abraham (Genesis 15:14, 'come out with great substance'), the just recompense for the long unpaid bondage (the oppressed enriched by the oppressors), accomplished by God's giving favor (not fraud); the wealth for the tabernacle (Exodus 35:5), a type of the spoiling of the strong man (Colossians 2:15; Luke 11:22)

themes: And the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians, so that they lent unto them such things as they required. And they spoiled the Egyptians
the spoiling of the Egyptians (the crux of the recompense)
the fulfillment of the promise to Abraham, the just recompense for the bondage (Genesis 15:14)
God's people enriched (the wealth for the tabernacle, Exodus 35:5), a type of the spoiling of the strong man (Colossians 2:15)

schools.summary: The journey begun and the great number of Israel: 'and the children of Israel journeyed from Rameses to Succoth, about six hundred thousand on foot that were men, beside children' — Israel setting out from Rameses (in Goshen, the place of their gathering) to Succoth (the first stage of the Exodus, the journey of the deliverance begun, the marching-out of the nation, the start of the wilderness journey); the 'six hundred thousand... men' (the men of fighting age, twenty and upward, Numbers 1:46, so the whole nation, with women and children, perhaps two million or more) — the great multitude (the seventy souls, Exodus 1:5, grown into a nation, the covenant promise fulfilled, Genesis 12:2; 46:3, 'I will make of thee a great nation,' the providence multiplying the seed); 'beside children' (the men alone six hundred thousand, besides women and children, the whole nation a vast host, the great nation marching out, the armies of the LORD, v.41, the providence, the small seed become a multitude, Deuteronomy 10:22) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And the children of Israel journeyed from Rameses to Succoth, about six hundred thousand on foot that were men, beside children
the journey begun and the great number of Israel
the small seed (seventy, Exodus 1:5) grown into a great nation (Genesis 12:2; 46:3; Deuteronomy 10:22)
the armies of the LORD marching out (v.41)

schools.summary: The mixed multitude going up with Israel: 'and a mixed multitude went up also with them; and flocks, and herds, even very much cattle' — a 'mixed multitude' (a crowd of non-Israelites, Egyptians or others, perhaps drawn by the wonders, or self-interest, or some faith) going up with Israel, along with the flocks, herds, and much cattle (the company of strangers, joined to Israel at the Exodus, the great train, the nation with its substance); the 'mixed multitude' (the strangers, of varied motives — some perhaps true converts, many drawn by the moment, who would later prove a snare, Numbers 11:4, 'the mixt multitude... fell a lusting') — the mingled company (the lesson, the visible church mingled with those of unsound heart, the tares among the wheat, Matthew 13:25-30); 'flocks, and herds, even very much cattle' (the great wealth of cattle, all brought out, not a hoof left, Exodus 10:26, the substance of the redeemed, the great train, the providence, the people enriched, going out with great substance, Genesis 15:14) — Keil and Delitzsch, JFB, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And a mixed multitude went up also with them; and flocks, and herds, even very much cattle
the mixed multitude going up with Israel
the strangers of varied motives, who would later prove a snare (Numbers 11:4)
the visible church mingled with the tares (Matthew 13:25-30)

schools.summary: The unleavened cakes of the haste: 'and they baked unleavened cakes of the dough which they brought forth out of Egypt, for it was not leavened; because they were thrust out of Egypt, and could not tarry, neither had they prepared for themselves any victual' — the people baking unleavened cakes of the dough they carried (v.34), for it had not leavened, because they were thrust out in haste and could not tarry (the unleavened bread, the memorial of the hasty deliverance, the feast of unleavened bread, Exodus 12:15-20; 13:3, the haste, no time to leaven or prepare provisions); the thrusting-out (the Egyptians driving them out, v.33; Exodus 11:1) — the haste of the departure (the deliverance so sudden no time remained); 'neither had they prepared for themselves any victual' (no provisions made, the suddenness of the departure, the people trusting God for their supply in the wilderness, the manna to come, Exodus 16, the providence, the haste, the unleavened bread, the memorial) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And they baked unleavened cakes of the dough which they brought forth out of Egypt, for it was not leavened; because they were thrust out of Egypt, and could not tarry
the unleavened cakes of the haste
the memorial of the hasty deliverance (Exodus 12:15; 13:3)
thrust out in haste, no time to prepare provisions (Exodus 11:1; 16)

schools.summary: The four hundred and thirty years of the sojourning: 'now the sojourning of the children of Israel, who dwelt in Egypt, was four hundred and thirty years' — the sojourning of Israel 430 years (the witnesses weighing the reckoning: the 430 years, the dominant reading holds, dating from the call of Abraham and the covenant, or from the promise to Abraham, Genesis 12; 15:13, the 'four hundred years' of affliction being a round number within the 430, not from the entrance into Egypt alone, for the time in Egypt proper was shorter, about 215 years, Paul confirming the 430 years from the promise to the law, Galatians 3:17, 'the covenant... the law, which was four hundred and thirty years after,' the period of the sojourning, Canaan and Egypt, to the Exodus); the phrase (the Septuagint and Samaritan add 'and in Canaan') covering the whole sojourning of the patriarchs and Israel (in Canaan and Egypt) — the 430 years from Abraham to the Exodus (the period reconciled with Galatians 3:17); 'four hundred and thirty years' (the exact period fulfilled, the promise to Abraham, Genesis 15:13, 'four hundred years,' the 430 to the day, v.41, the precise providence, God's word fulfilled to the very day, the covenant kept) — Calvin, Gill, Clarke, Wesley.

debates.question: From when are the 430 years reckoned?

sides.position: From the call/promise to Abraham (Genesis 12; 15:13) to the Exodus - the whole sojourning in Canaan and Egypt (the time in Egypt proper being shorter, ~215 years); confirmed by Paul, 'the covenant... the law, which was 430 years after' (Galatians 3:17)

themes: Now the sojourning of the children of Israel, who dwelt in Egypt, was four hundred and thirty years
the four hundred and thirty years of the sojourning
reckoned from the call/promise to Abraham (Genesis 12; 15:13), the whole sojourning in Canaan and Egypt
confirmed by Paul (Galatians 3:17), the covenant kept to the day

schools.summary: The deliverance on the selfsame day: 'and it came to pass at the end of the four hundred and thirty years, even the selfsame day it came to pass, that all the hosts of the LORD went out from the land of Egypt' — the great word — 'at the end of the four hundred and thirty years, even the selfsame day it came to pass': the deliverance coming at the exact end of the 430 years, on the very same day (the precise day the period closed, the exactness of God's providence, the promise fulfilled to the very day, not a day early or late, Genesis 15:13-16; Galatians 3:17, the punctual faithfulness of God, his word performed at the appointed time, Habakkuk 2:3, 'at the end it shall speak, and not lie,' the lesson — God's promises fulfilled to the day, his providence exact in its timing, Acts 7:17, 'when the time of the promise drew nigh'); 'the hosts of the LORD' (the armies of the LORD, the marshalled nation, by their hosts, v.51; Exodus 7:4) — Israel going out as ordered ranks, the armies of God (not a rabble of fleeing slaves, but the hosts of the LORD, marching out in order, the deliverance of the ordered nation, the LORD's own armies); the going-out on the selfsame day — the exact accomplishment of the deliverance (the covenant fulfilled, the redemption wrought, the providence, the punctual faithfulness of God, the redemption a foretaste of the great redemption in the fulness of time, Galatians 4:4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'even the selfsame day' display?

sides.position: The exactness of God's providence - the promise fulfilled to the very day (Genesis 15:13; Galatians 3:17), the punctual faithfulness of God performing his word at the appointed time (Habakkuk 2:3; Acts 7:17); 'the hosts of the LORD' marching out as the ordered armies of God, a foretaste of the redemption in the fulness of time (Galatians 4:4)

themes: And it came to pass at the end of the four hundred and thirty years, even the selfsame day it came to pass, that all the hosts of the LORD went out from the land of Egypt
the deliverance on the selfsame day (the crux of the precise fulfillment)
the promise fulfilled to the very day, the punctual faithfulness of God (Genesis 15:13; Galatians 3:17; Habakkuk 2:3)
'the hosts of the LORD' marching out as the ordered armies of God (a foretaste, Galatians 4:4)

schools.summary: The memorial night of the LORD: 'it is a night to be much observed unto the LORD for bringing them out from the land of Egypt: this is that night of the LORD to be observed of all the children of Israel in their generations' — the night of the deliverance (the Passover night, the going-out) to be 'much observed' (a night of solemn watching, kept and remembered) unto the LORD (the memorial night, the night of redemption, kept in all generations, the solemn commemoration of the deliverance); 'that night of the LORD' (the night peculiarly the LORD's, the night of his great work of redemption) — the night of the Passover and the Exodus (the night to be kept by Israel, the redemption-night); 'to be observed of all the children of Israel in their generations' (the night kept by all Israel, in every generation, the Passover, the perpetual ordinance, Exodus 12:14, 17, the memorial of the redemption, handed down through the generations, the providence, the deliverance remembered for ever, the type of the great redemption, the Lord's Supper, the new Passover, 1 Corinthians 5:7-8; 11:24-26, 'this do in remembrance of me') — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: It is a night to be much observed unto the LORD for bringing them out from the land of Egypt: this is that night of the LORD to be observed of all the children of Israel in their generations
the memorial night of the LORD
the night of redemption kept for ever, in all generations (Exodus 12:14)
the type of the great redemption, the new Passover (1 Corinthians 5:7-8; 11:24-26)

schools.summary: The ordinance of the Passover and its restriction: 'and the LORD said unto Moses and Aaron, This is the ordinance of the passover: There shall no stranger eat thereof' — God giving the ordinance (the rules, statute) of the Passover, regulating who might eat it (the law of the feast, the holy ordinance, to be kept according to God's appointment); 'no stranger shall eat thereof' — no 'stranger' (a foreigner, an alien, one not of the covenant, uncircumcised, v.48) might eat the Passover (the restriction to the covenant people, only the circumcised, the members of the covenant, vv.44-48, the holiness of the ordinance, not for the uncircumcised, the alien from the covenant, the lesson — the Passover, a type of Christ, 1 Corinthians 5:7, only for the covenant people, the circumcised in heart, the spiritual Israel, the believer, the unconverted have no part in Christ the Passover, until they are within the covenant by faith and the new birth, John 3:3, the ordinance of redemption only for the redeemed); the Passover restricted to the covenant — the holy feast guarded (the unworthy excluded, the providence, the type of the Lord's table, only for the members of Christ, the faithful, 1 Corinthians 11:27-29) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses and Aaron, This is the ordinance of the passover: There shall no stranger eat thereof
the ordinance of the Passover and its restriction
the Passover only for the covenant people, the circumcised (vv.44-48)
the type of Christ (1 Corinthians 5:7) and the Lord's table, only for the members of Christ (1 Corinthians 11:27-29)

schools.summary: The circumcised servant admitted to the Passover: 'but every man's servant that is bought for money, when thou hast circumcised him, then shall he eat thereof' — a bought servant (a slave, even of foreign origin), once circumcised (brought into the covenant), might eat the Passover (the admission by circumcision, the covenant sign, the door open to the stranger who entered the covenant, not by birth, but by the covenant sign, the inclusion of the circumcised); circumcision (the covenant sign, Genesis 17:10-13) admitting even the bought servant — the entrance into the covenant (the door for the stranger); 'then shall he eat thereof' (the circumcised servant admitted, within the covenant, partaking of the Passover, the lesson — the covenant open to all who enter by the sign, the Gentile brought near by the covenant, a foreshadowing of the calling of the Gentiles, Ephesians 2:12-13, the partaker of Christ the Passover, the one who is within the covenant by faith and the seal) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But every man's servant that is bought for money, when thou hast circumcised him, then shall he eat thereof
the circumcised servant admitted to the Passover
the covenant open to the stranger who enters by the sign (Genesis 17:10-13)
a foreshadowing of the calling of the Gentiles (Ephesians 2:12-13)

schools.summary: The uncircumcised excluded from the Passover: 'a foreigner and an hired servant shall not eat thereof' — a 'foreigner' (a sojourner, a temporary resident, not a covenant-member) and a 'hired servant' (a day-laborer, not bought into the household nor circumcised) might not eat the Passover (the exclusion of the uncircumcised, those outside the covenant, not bearing the sign, the restriction, the holy feast only for the covenant people, v.43, 48); the 'foreigner' (the uncircumcised alien, outside the covenant) — excluded from the Passover (the holy feast guarded, the one without the covenant sign); 'an hired servant shall not eat thereof' (the hired, uncircumcised, servant excluded, not within the covenant, the holiness of the ordinance, only for the circumcised, the covenant people, the lesson — the unconverted, the stranger to the covenant, have no part in Christ the Passover, 1 Corinthians 5:7, the Lord's table only for the faithful) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Clarke.

themes: A foreigner and an hired servant shall not eat thereof
the uncircumcised excluded from the Passover
the holy feast only for the covenant people, the uncircumcised barred (v.43)
the type of Christ only for the covenant people (1 Corinthians 5:7)

schools.summary: The Passover eaten in one house and no bone broken: 'in one house shall it be eaten; thou shalt not carry forth ought of the flesh abroad out of the house; neither shall ye break a bone thereof' — the Passover to be eaten in one house (the whole lamb, by the household together, not divided among houses, nor carried abroad, the unity of the feast, the one lamb, the one household, the unbroken company, the lesson, the unity of the church, the one body partaking of the one Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:17); the command — 'neither shall ye break a bone thereof': not a bone of the Passover lamb to be broken (the lamb roast whole, eaten whole, the bones unbroken, the witnesses with one voice noting the great fulfillment in Christ — 'for these things were done, that the scripture should be fulfilled, A bone of him shall not be broken,' John 19:36, quoting this verse and Numbers 9:12; Psalm 34:20: at the crucifixion, the soldiers braking the legs of the two thieves, but, finding Jesus already dead, braking not his legs — the Passover lamb's unbroken bone fulfilled in Christ, the true Passover, 1 Corinthians 5:7, 'Christ our passover is sacrificed for us,' the lamb without blemish, slain, its blood the shelter, its bones unbroken — the type openly fulfilled in the person of Christ, the providence ordering the very details of the type to point to the Lamb of God, John 1:29, the integrity of the Lamb, not a bone broken, the body whole, the type of the whole, undivided Christ); the unbroken bone — the wondrous type fulfilled in Christ (John 19:36, the Passover lamb the figure of the Lamb of God, the providence, the ancient ordinance pointing, in its smallest detail, to the cross) — Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'neither shall ye break a bone thereof' foreshadow?

sides.position: The Passover lamb's unbroken bone fulfilled openly in Christ the true Passover (John 19:36, 'A bone of him shall not be broken'; Numbers 9:12; Psalm 34:20; 1 Corinthians 5:7) - at the cross the soldiers brake not Jesus' legs, the type ordered in its smallest detail to the cross, the Lamb of God (John 1:29)

themes: In one house shall it be eaten... neither shall ye break a bone thereof
the Passover eaten in one house and no bone broken (the crux of the unbroken bone)
not a bone broken, fulfilled openly in Christ the true Passover (John 19:36; Numbers 9:12; Psalm 34:20; 1 Corinthians 5:7)
the type ordered in its smallest detail to the cross, the Lamb of God (John 1:29)

schools.summary: All Israel to keep the Passover: 'all the congregation of Israel shall keep it' — the whole congregation of Israel to keep the Passover (every household, the entire covenant people, the universal duty, none of the covenant exempt, the feast of all Israel, the redemption commemorated by the whole nation); the whole congregation (every Israelite household) — the universal observance (the feast of the covenant people); 'shall keep it' (the binding duty, the Passover to be kept by all, every year, Exodus 12:14, the obligation of the covenant people, the lesson — all the redeemed to keep the memorial, the whole church to partake of Christ the Passover, the Lord's table for all the faithful, 1 Corinthians 11:24-26) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: All the congregation of Israel shall keep it
all Israel to keep the Passover
the universal duty of the whole covenant people (Exodus 12:14)
all the redeemed to keep the memorial (1 Corinthians 11:24-26)

schools.summary: The stranger admitted by circumcision: 'and when a stranger shall sojourn with thee, and will keep the passover to the LORD, let all his males be circumcised, and then let him come near and keep it; and he shall be as one that is born in the land: for no uncircumcised person shall eat thereof' — a stranger (a Gentile sojourner) who would keep the Passover must first be circumcised (he and his males), entering the covenant, then he might keep it (the door open to the Gentile by the covenant sign, the admission of the stranger); the circumcised stranger 'shall be as one that is born in the land' — fully equal to the native Israelite (the covenant making no difference between the born Israelite and the proselyte, the inclusion of the Gentile, a foreshadowing of the calling of the Gentiles, the breaking-down of the middle wall, Ephesians 2:11-19, 'neither Jew nor Greek,' Galatians 3:28, the stranger made fellow-citizen); 'no uncircumcised person shall eat thereof' (the covenant sign required for all, the uncircumcised excluded, whether Israelite or stranger, the holiness of the ordinance, the lesson — only the covenant-member, the circumcised in heart, the believer, may partake of Christ the Passover, the door open to all who enter by the covenant) — Gill, Wesley, Clarke.

themes: And when a stranger shall sojourn with thee, and will keep the passover to the LORD, let all his males be circumcised... and he shall be as one that is born in the land
the stranger admitted by circumcision
the circumcised stranger equal to the native Israelite
a foreshadowing of the calling of the Gentiles (Ephesians 2:11-19; Galatians 3:28)

schools.summary: One law for the native and the stranger: 'one law shall be to him that is homeborn, and unto the stranger that sojourneth among you' — one and the same law (regarding the Passover, and the covenant) applying to the native Israelite and the circumcised stranger (the equality of the covenant, no different rule for the proselyte, the inclusion of the Gentile, one law, one people, one covenant); 'one law' (the same for native and stranger) — the unity of the covenant people (the equality of the proselyte, the lesson, the breaking-down of the partition, the Gentiles made one with Israel in Christ, Ephesians 2:14-15, 'there is neither Jew nor Greek,' Galatians 3:28; Colossians 3:11); 'him that is homeborn, and unto the stranger' (the native and the stranger under one law, the covenant open to all who enter, the foreshadowing of the one church of Jew and Gentile, one body in Christ, Ephesians 2:16, the one new man, the providence, the inclusion of the nations in the covenant of grace) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: One law shall be to him that is homeborn, and unto the stranger that sojourneth among you
one law for the native and the stranger
the equality of the proselyte, the unity of the covenant people
a foreshadowing of the one church of Jew and Gentile in Christ (Ephesians 2:14-16; Galatians 3:28)

schools.summary: Israel's full obedience: 'thus did all the children of Israel; as the LORD commanded Moses and Aaron, so did they' — all Israel obeying, keeping the Passover and its ordinances exactly as the LORD commanded (the full, universal obedience, the faith that kept the ordinance, the people doing all as appointed); the obedience (as commanded) — the faithful keeping (the people obeying); 'so did they' (the exact, full obedience, all as commanded, the keeping of the Passover, the faith of the redeemed, Hebrews 11:28, the providence, the obedience that secured the deliverance, vv.51) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Thus did all the children of Israel; as the LORD commanded Moses and Aaron, so did they
Israel's full obedience
the full, universal obedience to the ordinance
the faith of the redeemed that kept the Passover (Hebrews 11:28)

schools.summary: The deliverance accomplished on the selfsame day: 'and it came to pass the selfsame day, that the LORD did bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt by their armies' — on the very day (the end of the 430 years, v.41), the LORD bringing Israel out of Egypt (the deliverance accomplished, the redemption wrought, the bondage ended, the LORD the author, he brought them out, by his strong hand, the Exodus complete); 'the LORD did bring them out' — God the deliverer (the redemption his work, by his power, Exodus 6:6; Deuteronomy 4:34, the strong hand that freed them, the LORD's own work); 'by their armies' (Israel brought out as ordered hosts, the armies of the LORD, v.41; Exodus 7:4, the marshalled nation, in their tribes and ranks, the providence, the deliverance complete, the redemption accomplished, the new chapter of the nation's life begun, ch. 13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass the selfsame day, that the LORD did bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt by their armies
the deliverance accomplished on the selfsame day
the redemption wrought on the very day, the LORD's own work (v.41; Exodus 6:6)
Israel brought out as the ordered armies of the LORD (Exodus 7:4)
